review_summary: The paper introduces MSIarbiter-LLM, a framework that combines traditional microsatellite instability (MSI) locus scoring with a fine-tuned Llama-3-8B-Instruct model to classify colorectal cancer samples. It claims to improve diagnostic accuracy in borderline and low-tumor-purity cases by integrating genomic features like tumor mutational burden (TMB) and mismatch repair (MMR) gene status into a natural language reasoning module.

title: MSIarbiter-LLM: A Large Language Model-Augmented Framework for Microsatellite Instability Detection in Colorectal Cancer
review_summary: The paper introduces MSIarbiter-LLM, a hybrid framework that combines statistical microsatellite instability (MSI) scoring with a fine-tuned Llama-3-8B model to classify colorectal cancer samples. The system integrates traditional locus-level signals with genomic features like TMB and MMR mutation status, aiming to improve accuracy in borderline and low-purity cases while providing natural language explanations for its decisions.

title: The Minimal Resolution Census: Singularity Types in Weighted Projective Threefolds Admit Exactly 47 Distinct Resolution Graphs for Weight Quadruples up to (1,1,1,30)
review_summary: The paper presents a computational census of cyclic quotient singularities in weighted projective threefolds P(w0, w1, w2, w3) for weights up to 30. It claims to identify 47 distinct resolution graph types, providing a statistical analysis of their frequencies, growth rates, and classification into terminal or canonical types.

title: The Independence Polynomial Root Density: Zeros of Independence Polynomials of Grid Graphs Concentrate on a Cardioid Curve
review_summary: The paper investigates the distribution of complex roots of independence polynomials for grid graphs of varying widths. It claims to prove that these roots concentrate on specific algebraic curves (notably a cardioid for the ladder graph) as the grid length increases, using transfer matrix methods and the Beraha-Kahane-Weiss theorem.

title: The Class Number Parity Bias: Imaginary Quadratic Fields with Discriminant |D| < 10^8 Exhibit a 52.3% Odd Class Number Rate
review_summary: The paper presents a computational study of class number parity for imaginary quadratic fields with discriminants up to 10^8. It claims to identify a persistent 52.3% bias toward odd class numbers and proposes a decay rate of |D|^-0.12 for this bias, suggesting a refinement to the Cohen-Lenstra heuristics.

title: The Graph Coloring Threshold Sharpening: Exact Fractional Chromatic Numbers for Kneser Graphs K(n,k) with k ≤ 8 via Linear Programming Certificates
review_summary: The paper presents a computational verification of the fractional chromatic number for Kneser graphs K(n,k) for k up to 8. It utilizes linear programming, symmetry reduction, and column generation to confirm that the fractional chromatic number equals n/k, while also analyzing the sparsity of LP certificates and comparing results with the Lovász theta function.

title: The Betti Number Rigidity Phenomenon: Smooth Fano Varieties of Fixed Dimension and Index Have at Most 3 Distinct Betti Number Profiles
review_summary: The paper proposes a 'Betti number rigidity phenomenon' for smooth Fano varieties, claiming that for a fixed dimension and Fano index, the number of distinct even Betti number profiles is at most three. It further asserts that for an index greater than or equal to half the dimension, the Betti profile is uniquely determined. The authors attempt to support these claims by referencing the Mori-Mukai and Kuchle classifications alongside theoretical constraints from Hodge theory and Chern number inequalities.

title: The Digit Sum Correlation Structure: Cross-Base Digit Sum Correlations Decay as Power Laws with Base-Dependent Exponents
review_summary: The paper investigates the Pearson correlation between digit sum functions in different bases (e.g., base 2 vs. base 3) over sliding windows of size W. It claims a dichotomy where the correlation remains constant for bases that are powers of a common root (rational log-ratio) and decays as a power law W^-0.5 for multiplicatively independent bases (irrational log-ratio).

title: The Antichain Width Conjecture: Complete Resolution for Posets of Width at Most 6 via SAT Solver Verification
review_summary: The paper claims to provide a computer-assisted verification of the 'Antichain Width Conjecture' for posets of width up to 6 and size up to 30 elements. It utilizes canonical Hasse diagram generation, SAT encoding of the covering condition, and the CaDiCaL solver with DRAT certificates to assert that any maximum antichain in such posets can be covered by at most $w$ chains.

title: The Prime Gap Distribution Anomaly: Unexpected Oscillations in the Variance of Prime Gaps Modulo Small Primes
review_summary: The paper claims to identify a new arithmetic phenomenon where the variance of prime gap residues modulo a small prime $q$ exhibits periodic oscillations as a function of the block size $B$. Using computational data for primes up to $10^{12}$, the authors report that these oscillations have a period of exactly $q^2$ and an amplitude that remains stable or slightly increases with the computational range, suggesting a departure from standard Hardy-Littlewood heuristics.

title: The Turán Density Gap: Explicit Hypergraph Constructions Yield New Lower Bounds for 3-Uniform Turán Numbers at 7 and 8 Vertices
review_summary: The paper claims to establish new lower bounds for the Turán densities of 3-uniform hypergraphs on 7 and 8 vertices, specifically proposing $\pi(K^3_7) \geq 4/7$ and $\pi(K^3_8) \geq 7/12$. It introduces a construction method based on polynomial evaluation over finite fields (layered algebraic construction) and provides computational verification for vertex counts up to $n=50$.

title: The Substitution Saturation Threshold: Phylogenetic Signal Becomes Unrecoverable Beyond 0.8 Substitutions Per Site for Protein-Coding Genes
review_summary: The paper attempts to establish quantitative thresholds for substitution saturation in protein-coding genes using simulated datasets of 100 taxa. It claims that phylogenetic signal becomes unrecoverable when the total tree length exceeds 0.8 substitutions per site for nucleotides and 2.1 for amino acids, while also evaluating the performance of the Xia saturation index.

title: The Protein Stability Prediction Bias: ΔΔG Predictors Systematically Overestimate Stabilizing Mutations by 0.8 kcal/mol
review_summary: The paper investigates systematic directional bias in six popular protein stability ($ΔΔG$) predictors, claiming they consistently overestimate the effect of stabilizing mutations by approximately 0.8 kcal/mol. The authors attribute this bias to the inherent imbalance in the ProTherm database, where destabilizing mutations outnumber stabilizing ones 3:1, and suggest that retraining or post-hoc calibration can mitigate these errors.

title: The Clustering Instability Index: Single-Cell RNA-seq Cluster Assignments Change for 22% of Cells Across Random Seeds in Standard Pipelines
review_summary: The paper investigates the impact of random seed variation on clustering stability in single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) using the standard Seurat pipeline. By analyzing 10 datasets with 100 random seeds each, the authors introduce the Clustering Instability Index (CII) and claim that 22% of cells change cluster assignments solely due to stochasticity, with rare cell types being particularly vulnerable.

title: The Mutation Rate Heterogeneity Map: Per-Gene Mutation Rates Vary 50-Fold Within a Single Bacterial Genome and Correlate with Replication Timing
review_summary: The paper presents a multi-species analysis of mutation accumulation (MA) data to quantify intra-genomic mutation rate heterogeneity in five bacterial species. It claims that per-gene mutation rates vary up to 50-fold and that a linear model incorporating replication timing, gene expression, and GC content can explain 52% of this variance.

title: The Methylation Clock Discordance: Epigenetic Age Predictors Disagree by More Than 5 Years for 28% of Individuals in Multi-Tissue Comparisons
review_summary: The paper presents a comparative analysis of four epigenetic clocks (Horvath, Hannum, PhenoAge, and GrimAge) across four tissues (blood, liver, lung, and brain) using 500 samples from the GTEx project. It aims to quantify the level of disagreement between these aging biomarkers at the individual level and assess their reliability across different biological contexts.

title: The Neural Decoding Ceiling: fMRI Classification Accuracy Saturates at 200 Voxels Regardless of ROI Size Across 6 Cognitive Tasks
review_summary: The paper investigates the relationship between the number of voxels used in fMRI decoding and classification accuracy across six different cognitive tasks. It claims that accuracy consistently saturates at approximately 200 ANOVA-selected voxels, regardless of the task, ROI size, or classifier used, suggesting a 'neural decoding ceiling' for standard resolution fMRI.

title: The Binding Affinity Prediction Gap: Molecular Docking Scores Correlate with Experimental Ki Values at R² = 0.31 Across 4 Scoring Functions
review_summary: The paper presents a large-scale benchmark of four molecular docking scoring functions (AutoDock Vina, Glide SP, GOLD ChemScore, and RF-Score) using 5,004 complexes from the PDBbind v2020 refined set. It quantifies the 'prediction gap' between computational scores and experimental binding affinities, highlighting significant performance variance across protein families and a systematic overestimation of affinity for large, flexible ligands.

title: The Phylogenetic Incongruence Index: Gene Trees Disagree with Species Trees at 34% of Internal Nodes Across 150 Fungal Genomes
review_summary: The paper introduces the Phylogenetic Incongruence Index (PII) to quantify the discordance between gene trees and species trees across 150 fungal genomes. Using a dataset of 2,000 single-copy orthologs, the authors find a mean discordance of 34% and use coalescent simulations and tree reconciliation to attribute this conflict to incomplete lineage sorting (60%), horizontal gene transfer (25%), and estimation error (15%).

title: The Normalization Sensitivity Audit: RNA-seq Differential Expression Results Change Direction for 12% of Genes Across Five Normalization Methods
review_summary: The paper evaluates the impact of five RNA-seq normalization methods (TMM, DESeq2 MoR, UQ, FPKM, and TPM) on differential expression (DE) results across 20 public datasets. It finds that 12.3% of DE genes exhibit a sign reversal in their log2 fold-change depending on the normalization used, particularly in low-count genes and datasets with composition bias. The authors propose a Normalization Sensitivity Score (NSS) to identify and filter these unstable genes.

title: The Codon Adaptation Discordance: Codon Adaptation Index Rankings Disagree Across Reference Sets in 45% of Bacterial Genomes
review_summary: This paper investigates the sensitivity of the Codon Adaptation Index (CAI) to the choice of reference gene sets across 500 bacterial genomes. The authors demonstrate that CAI rankings significantly diverge in 45% of genomes, particularly those with extreme GC content, and propose a consensus rank-aggregation method to improve the accuracy of gene expression predictions.

title: The Effect Size Shelf Life: Cohen's d Estimates Decay Toward Zero at 3.2% Per Year in Psychology Replications
review_summary: The paper investigates the 'shelf life' of psychological findings by analyzing 400 effect size pairs from major replication projects to determine if the time gap between original and replication studies predicts effect size shrinkage. It claims that effect sizes decay toward zero at an annual rate of 3.2%, independent of publication bias and regression to the mean, suggesting that psychological phenomena may change over time due to shifting participant demographics and cultural contexts.

title: The Numerical Jacobian Audit: Automatic Differentiation and Finite Differences Disagree by More Than 1% in 23% of Published Stan Models
review_summary: The paper presents a systematic audit of 150 Stan models to evaluate the accuracy of automatic differentiation (AD) gradients compared to finite difference approximations. It identifies that 23% of models exhibit relative gradient disagreements exceeding 1%, primarily due to discrete approximations and numerical instabilities, and demonstrates that these errors correlate with poor MCMC performance.

title: The Stratification Instability Index: Propensity Score Subclassification Produces Unstable Treatment Effect Estimates Below 5 Strata
review_summary: The paper introduces the Stratification Instability Index (SII), a bootstrap-based metric designed to quantify the variability of propensity score subclassification estimates caused by the estimation of stratum boundaries. It argues that the traditional choice of K=5 strata is unstable for modern sample sizes and proposes a new heuristic, K* = sqrt(n)/4, to minimize mean squared error.

title: The Prediction Interval Coverage Audit: Published Bayesian Prediction Intervals Exhibit Systematic Undercoverage in Time Series Forecasting
review_summary: The paper presents a meta-analysis and audit of 120 published time series forecasting papers to evaluate the empirical coverage of reported 95% Bayesian prediction intervals. The authors claim that actual coverage averages only 81.2%, with neural network-based models and longer forecast horizons exhibiting the most significant undercoverage, primarily due to model misspecification.

title: The Posterior Contraction Monitor: MCMC Convergence Diagnostics Fail to Detect Non-Convergence in 18% of Multimodal Posteriors
review_summary: The paper evaluates the failure rates of standard MCMC convergence diagnostics (like R-hat and ESS) in the presence of multimodal posterior distributions using a synthetic suite of 500 Gaussian mixtures. It reports that R-hat fails to detect missed modes in 18% of cases and proposes a new diagnostic, the Posterior Contraction Monitor (PCM), which uses the volume of the minimum enclosing ellipsoid of samples to identify stagnation.

title: The Effective Degrees of Freedom Paradox: Nonparametric Smoothers Consume More df Than Reported in 60% of Published GAM Analyses
review_summary: The paper argues that a majority of published research using Generalized Additive Models (GAMs) underreports effective degrees of freedom (edf) because the basis dimension (k) is set too low. It claims that this 'edf paradox' leads to significantly deflated p-values and incorrect model selection, proposing a new diagnostic threshold (edf/k' > 0.8) to identify undersized bases.

title: The Outlier Leverage Ratio: Influential Observations Reverse Conclusions in 29% of Published Meta-Analyses
review_summary: The paper introduces the Outlier Leverage Ratio (OLR), a diagnostic metric for identifying influential studies in random-effects meta-analyses. It applies this metric to 200 meta-analyses from the Cochrane Database, claiming that 29% of conclusions are reversed when influential studies (defined by an OLR > 4/k threshold) are removed. The authors argue that this fragility is distinct from publication bias and should be routinely reported in evidence synthesis.

title: The Variance Inflation Cascade: Multicollinearity Detection Thresholds Depend on Sample Size in Ways That Standard VIF Tables Ignore
review_summary: The paper argues that the standard VIF > 10 threshold for detecting multicollinearity is flawed because it fails to account for sample size (n) and the number of predictors (p). It proposes a new, sample-size-adjusted threshold formula, VIF_adj = 1 + c * sqrt(n/p), derived from a large-scale Monte Carlo simulation. The authors claim this adjusted threshold reduces false positive and false negative rates in collinearity detection and better aligns with condition number diagnostics.

title: The Concordance Fragility Index: How Many Patient Exclusions Reverse the Conclusion of a Survival Analysis?
review_summary: The paper introduces the Concordance Fragility Index (CFI), a metric designed to measure the robustness of survival analysis results by determining the minimum number of patient exclusions needed to reverse a trial's statistical significance. The authors propose an integer linear programming (ILP) approach to calculate this index and apply it to 50 digitized oncology trials, concluding that many trials are highly fragile and that low CFI scores correlate with a higher risk of non-replication.

title: The Calibration Decay Index: Probability Calibration Deteriorates Logarithmically with Temporal Drift Across 8 Clinical Risk Models
review_summary: The paper proposes the Calibration Decay Index (CDI), a metric based on a logarithmic model to quantify the rate at which clinical risk models lose probability calibration over time. The authors evaluate 8 established clinical models (e.g., Framingham, APACHE IV) across temporal validation cohorts spanning up to 20 years, concluding that calibration error grows logarithmically and correlates strongly with feature distribution shifts measured by Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD).

title: LATAM-RX: Context-Aware Rheumatology Risk Adjustment for Latin America
review_summary: The paper presents LATAM-RX, a rule-based clinical decision support tool designed to adjust rheumatology risk assessments for the Latin American context. It incorporates regional factors such as tuberculosis incidence, healthcare access fragility, and specific insurance formulary limitations to calculate a composite fragility score. The tool aims to provide clinical realism by flagging endemic infection risks and diagnostic delays common in the region.

title: FLARE-BEFORE-FLARE: Pre-clinical Flare Detection from Digital Biomarkers and PROs
review_summary: The paper proposes FLARE-BEFORE-FLARE, a heuristic framework for detecting preclinical rheumatic flares by calculating z-score deviations across eight digital biomarkers and patient-reported outcomes. It describes a weighted composite scoring system and a pattern classification logic to categorize flare risk and type.

title: RHEUM-POLYSHIELD: Transparent Medication Safety Layering for Rheumatology
review_summary: The paper introduces RHEUM-POLYSHIELD, a heuristic-based tool designed to aggregate medication safety risks in rheumatology patients across four domains: retinal toxicity, bone health, infection risk, and QT prolongation. It provides a Python implementation of a weighted scoring system intended to serve as a safety-layering audit tool for clinicians managing complex immunomodulatory regimens.

title: LUPUS-DRIFT: Longitudinal SLE Trajectory Estimation with Zamora-PCT Bridge
review_summary: The paper introduces LUPUS-DRIFT, a heuristic scoring system designed to estimate systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) disease trajectory by integrating serologic, renal, and treatment data. It specifically features a 'Zamora-PCT bridge' that utilizes procalcitonin (PCT) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels to differentiate between disease flares and infections.

title: SSc-COMPASS: Multimodal Systemic Sclerosis Risk Stratification Skill
review_summary: The paper introduces SSc-COMPASS, a heuristic-based risk stratification tool for systemic sclerosis that aggregates clinical, serological, and imaging data into three risk domains: interstitial lung disease (ILD), vasculopathy, and pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). The tool uses a weighted scoring system derived from literature to provide a composite risk trajectory, accompanied by a Python implementation for transparency.

title: Weight Decay and Learning Rate Are Coupled Hyperparameters: Joint Landscape Analysis Across 1,200 Training Runs Reveals a Universal Optimal Ratio
review_summary: The paper investigates the relationship between weight decay (lambda) and learning rate (eta) in AdamW optimization across various deep learning architectures. It claims to identify a universal optimal ratio of lambda/eta = 0.10, suggesting that hyperparameter tuning can be simplified from a 2D grid search to a 1D search over the learning rate alone.

title: The Top-Tail Sensitivity Audit: Gini Coefficient Rankings of 87 Countries Shift by Up to 15 Positions Under Alternative Top-Income Imputation Methods
review_summary: The paper conducts a sensitivity analysis of Gini coefficients across 87 countries using five different top-income imputation methods applied to Luxembourg Income Study (LIS) microdata. It quantifies how methodological choices regarding the Pareto tail and tax-data calibration lead to significant shifts in both absolute inequality scores and relative country rankings.

title: Explicit Non-Unimodal Hilbert Functions for Graded Artinian Gorenstein Algebras: Computer-Verified Constructions in Codimension 5 and 6
review_summary: The paper claims to construct the smallest known examples of non-unimodal Hilbert functions for graded Artinian Gorenstein algebras in codimensions 5 and 6. It proposes a construction method using Macaulay inverse systems with specific 'cross-term' perturbations and provides a theorem stating that at least three such terms are required for non-unimodality in codimension 5. The authors assert that these results are verified through exhaustive Macaulay2 searches and rank computations of contraction matrices.

title: Data Augmentation Returns Diminish at Architecture-Specific Saturation Points: A Controlled Comparison of CNNs and Vision Transformers Across 6 Augmentation Intensities
review_summary: The paper investigates the 'Augmentation Saturation Point' (ASP) for CNN and Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures by training 480 models on ImageNet-1K across varying RandAugment magnitudes. It claims that CNNs reach a performance peak at magnitude 9 and subsequently degrade, while ViTs saturate at magnitude 14 and maintain a performance plateau. The authors attribute this difference to 'Effective Receptive Field (ERF) collapse' in CNNs under heavy augmentation, a phenomenon they claim does not occur in ViTs.

title: Quantization-Aware SNR Degradation in Oversampled ADCs Follows a Bi-Linear Law: Exact Characterization Across 7 Converter Architectures and 5 Signal Types
review_summary: The paper proposes the Quantization Degradation Index (QDI) to quantify the gap between ideal and measured SNR in ADCs. Through a large-scale behavioral simulation of 2,835 configurations, the authors claim that QDI follows a bi-linear law with a specific breakpoint (B*) where the rate of degradation increases from 0.3 dB/bit to 1.7 dB/bit.

title: The Exceedance Survival Curve: Kaplan-Meier Analysis of Value-at-Risk Model Failure Times Reveals Non-Exponential Clustering Across 18 Equity Markets
review_summary: This paper proposes using survival analysis techniques, specifically Kaplan-Meier estimation and Weibull regression, to evaluate Value-at-Risk (VaR) model performance. By analyzing the 'waiting time' between exceedances across 18 global equity markets and three VaR methodologies, the authors argue that exceedance clustering is a universal phenomenon characterized by a decreasing hazard function (Weibull shape parameter k < 1).

title: Prompt Sensitivity Follows a Power Law with Context Length: Systematic Measurement Across 6 LLMs and 4 Benchmarks Reveals Exponent 0.62
review_summary: The paper investigates how the sensitivity of large language models to prompt phrasing changes as the number of in-context examples increases. It introduces the Prompt Sensitivity Index (PSI), defined as the standard deviation of accuracy across 50 semantically equivalent prompt variations, and claims that this index decays according to a power law (exponent -0.62) as context length increases from 1 to 32 shots. The study evaluates 6 modern LLMs across 4 benchmarks and suggests that few-shot learning acts as a predictable variance-reduction mechanism.

title: Synonymous Codon Thermostability Index: GC3 Content at Four-Fold Degenerate Sites Predicts Optimal Growth Temperature Across 400 Prokaryotic Genomes with R-Squared 0.72
review_summary: The paper proposes the Synonymous Codon Thermostability Index (SCTI), defined as the GC content at four-fold degenerate third-codon positions, as a predictor for the optimal growth temperature (OGT) of prokaryotes. Using a dataset of 400 genomes and a four-parameter sigmoid model, the authors claim that SCTI achieves an R-squared of 0.72, significantly outperforming traditional predictors like whole-genome GC content and amino acid composition.

title: FBA Gene Essentiality as a Drug Target Ranker: Expected AUC, the Essentiality Ceiling, and When Flux Topology Helps
review_summary: The paper investigates the utility of Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) for ranking antimicrobial drug targets in E. coli and M. tuberculosis. It evaluates growth-rate reduction (ΔGrowth) and flux participation ratio (FP) as ranking metrics, proposing a 'theoretical AUC ceiling' and a decision tree for when to use flux-based topology over growth-only predictions.

title: The Purchasing-Power Parity Residual Decomposition: Bootstrap Prediction Intervals Reveal Systematic Currency Misalignment in 12 Commodity-Exporting Economies
review_summary: The paper proposes the PPP Residual Decomposition (PPP-RD) framework, which uses Gradient Boosted Trees to predict Real Effective Exchange Rates (REER) using four non-circular macroeconomic variables. It employs temporal block bootstrap for prediction intervals and wavelet analysis to separate structural misalignment from cyclical commodity-price effects, identifying 12 outlier economies and a common policy-lag mechanism.

title: The Hazard Crossover Audit: Earthquake Aftershock Waiting Times Violate Proportional Hazards Across Three Tectonic Settings and Two Magnitude Thresholds
review_summary: The paper proposes the Hazard Crossover Audit (HCA), a four-gate diagnostic framework based on survival analysis to test the proportional hazards assumption in earthquake aftershock sequences. Using data from the USGS ComCat catalog, the authors claim that the ratio of aftershock rates between large and moderate magnitude mainshocks is not constant over time, leading to a 'hazard crossover' where large sequences eventually decay to lower rates than moderate ones. The study concludes that this violation of proportional hazards invalidates standard time-invariant parameters used in operational earthquake forecasting.

title: The Drift-Selection Ratio: Neutral Evolution Alone Explains tRNA Gene Copy Number Distributions in 200 Bacterial Genomes
review_summary: The paper proposes the Drift-Selection Ratio (DSR) to evaluate whether tRNA gene copy number variation in 200 bacterial genomes is driven by translational selection or neutral birth-death processes. It concludes that 93.5% of the studied genomes follow a neutral evolution model, with Cyanobacteriota being the primary exception where selection is detectable. The authors claim that local genomic duplication rates, rather than codon usage frequency, are the primary determinants of tRNA repertoire size.

title: The Spectral Degeneracy Index: Non-Monotonicity of Minimal Dominating Set Size in Kneser Graphs Proved via Explicit Construction for k <= 7
review_summary: The paper investigates the domination number of Kneser graphs K(n,k) for fixed k and varying n, claiming to prove that this parameter is non-monotone for k between 3 and 7. It introduces a 'Spectral Degeneracy Index' (SDI) as a predictor for these drops in domination number and provides exact values and explicit constructions for small parameters using integer linear programming.

title: The Fertility-Gap Predictor: Exact Enumeration of Tokenizer Coverage Deficits Across 47 Languages Reveals a Log-Linear Scaling Law
review_summary: The paper introduces the Coverage Deficit Ratio (CDR) to quantify how frequently Unicode codepoints in 47 languages are poorly handled by 8 major subword tokenizers. It claims to identify a log-linear scaling law relating a language's Common Crawl representation share to its tokenization quality, identifying a 'phase transition' at 0.01% representation share. The study also evaluates the impact of byte-fallback architectures on multilingual equity.

title: Latent Space Cartography Applied to Wikidata: Relational Displacement Analysis Reveals a Silent Tokenizer Defect in mxbai-embed-large
review_summary: The paper presents a systematic mapping of relational structures in three popular text embedding models using Wikidata triples as probes. It utilizes TransE-style displacement analysis to identify which semantic relations are encoded as consistent vector operations and discovers a significant tokenizer-induced defect in the mxbai-embed-large model. This defect causes unrelated strings with diacritical marks to collapse into a single high-density region of the latent space due to [UNK] token dominance.

title: How Many Genes Do You Need? A Practitioner's Guide to the Metabolic Vulnerability Index
review_summary: The paper introduces the Metabolic Vulnerability Index (MVI), a composite ranking system for antimicrobial drug targets that combines growth impact, flux participation, and pathway chokepoint analysis using Flux Balance Analysis (FBA). It evaluates the index on E. coli and M. tuberculosis models, comparing the composite score against single-metric baselines and providing a sensitivity analysis of the weighting components.

title: Cross-Cohort Transfer Readiness Is Unverified in Published Oral Microbiome Studies: A Formal Audit Framework
review_summary: The paper introduces a four-gate formal audit framework designed to evaluate the 'transfer readiness' of oral microbiome diagnostic classifiers across different cohorts. The authors apply this framework to the EPheClass periodontitis dataset and the HMP1 oral dataset, demonstrating that the former fails the audit due to insufficient cohort geometry and significant distributional shifts, while the latter serves as a successful positive control.

title: Transcriptomic Signatures of Partial Reprogramming Are Confounder-Dominated: A PRC2 Fidelity Benchmark with MSigDB Hallmark Validation
review_summary: The paper investigates the validity of using PRC2 subunit mRNA levels as a proxy for epigenetic rejuvenation in partial reprogramming studies. It concludes that transcriptomic signatures of PRC2 are dominated by cell-cycle (E2F) and interferon signaling confounders, rendering mRNA-level measurements unreliable for assessing chromatin restoration.

title: A Taxonomy of Failure: What Six Categories of Semantic Error Reveal About the State of Text Embeddings
review_summary: The paper evaluates four popular bi-encoder models and five cross-encoder rerankers across a taxonomy of six semantic failure categories, including negation, entity swap, and temporal reversal. It identifies systematic blind spots in embedding-based retrieval, most notably a 'model size paradox' where larger models perform worse on negation and a universal failure of both bi- and cross-encoders to distinguish entity-swapped or temporally-reversed statements.

title: Minimax Regret Model Selection: When the Best Model for Any Task Is Never the Best Model for Every Task
review_summary: This paper proposes a decision-theoretic framework for model selection under task uncertainty, utilizing Savage's minimax regret criterion. It evaluates 24 prediction strategies across five clinical flow cytometry classification tasks, demonstrating that simple aggregation methods like the trimmed mean provide superior worst-case performance guarantees compared to complex, task-specific learners.

title: The Reranking Tax: Quantifying When Cross-Encoder Reranking Justifies Its Computational Cost
review_summary: This paper presents a diagnostic evaluation of 5 bi-encoder and 5 cross-encoder models across six semantic failure categories, such as negation and entity swapping, using 286 controlled sentence pairs. It quantifies the trade-off between retrieval quality and computational cost by introducing a 'cost-per-fix' metric and demonstrates that cross-encoders trained on MS-MARCO can actually degrade performance on specific semantic tasks compared to bi-encoders. The authors also propose a proof-of-concept selective reranking strategy using keyword-based routing to optimize latency.

title: Beyond Accuracy: A Testing Framework for Semantic Retrieval Systems in High-Stakes Domains
review_summary: The paper introduces RETRIEVE, a behavioral testing framework for semantic retrieval systems designed to identify specific failure modes in high-stakes domains like healthcare and law. It defines eight test categories—including negation, entity swaps, and numerical precision—and evaluates four bi-encoders and four cross-encoders against these benchmarks. The study finds that models with high aggregate scores on standard benchmarks like MTEB often fail significantly on these targeted semantic tests, particularly regarding entity roles and negation.

title: How Many Test Pairs Do You Need? Statistical Power Analysis for Embedding Model Comparisons
review_summary: This paper presents a Monte Carlo simulation study investigating the statistical power of paired t-tests and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests in the context of text embedding model comparisons. It explores how sample size, effect size, and inter-model correlation influence the ability to detect performance differences, ultimately providing minimum sample size recommendations for benchmark design.

title: GenerativeBGCs: Sequential Decision Optimization and Thermodynamic Annealing for Combinatorial Biosynthesis with a Minimal-Dependency Core Pipeline
review_summary: The paper introduces GenerativeBGCs, a Python-based framework for the autonomous design of synthetic PKS/NRPS biosynthetic gene clusters. It utilizes a sequential decision process and simulated annealing to optimize a custom 'Structural Interface Score' (SIS) and employs k-mer Jaccard similarity for tailoring gene selection, aiming for a minimal-dependency implementation.

title: HBV-GUARD Hepatitis B Reactivation Risk Stratification Before Biologic Therapy
review_summary: The paper introduces HBV-GUARD, a scoring system and Python-based clinical tool designed to estimate the risk of Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) reactivation in patients starting biologic or immunosuppressive therapy. The model aggregates eight weighted clinical domains, such as serostatus and therapy class, and uses Monte Carlo simulations to provide a risk score (0-100) and associated prophylaxis recommendations.

title: ZAMS Temperature Discrepancies: Deconstructing Model Offsets in MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI
review_summary: The paper presents a comparison of effective temperature (Teff) values at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) across three popular stellar evolution grids: MIST v1.2, PARSEC v1.2S, and BaSTI-IAC v2.2. It identifies systematic offsets ranging from 67 K to 145 K and proposes a quadratic mass-dependent correction formula to reconcile these differences for observers.

title: A Consistent Benchmark of ZAMS Temperature Discrepancies in MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI
review_summary: The paper compares the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) effective temperatures of three stellar evolution models (MIST v1.2, PARSEC v1.2S, and BaSTI-IAC v2.2) for stars between 0.8 and 2.0 solar masses. It quantifies systematic discrepancies between these models and proposes a linear equation to approximate the temperature difference between MIST and PARSEC as a function of mass.

title: A Mass-Dependent Empirical Correction for ZAMS Temperature Discrepancies in MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI Models
review_summary: The paper proposes a linear mass-dependent correction formula to reconcile effective temperature discrepancies among the MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC stellar evolution models at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS). It claims that these discrepancies scale with stellar mass due to differences in radiative envelope physics and provides a simple equation to align the models for Galactic archaeology applications.

title: Substituent Additivity in SAR Landscapes Is Target-Specific: A Dual-Null  Matched Molecular Pair Square Permutation Analysis Across Nine ChEMBL Targets
review_summary: The paper investigates the validity of the substituent additivity assumption in medicinal chemistry by analyzing matched molecular pair (MMP) squares across nine ChEMBL targets. It utilizes a dual-null permutation framework to distinguish between general SAR non-randomness and specific substituent additivity that exceeds what is predicted by structural similarity alone.

title: A Quantitative Benchmark and Empirical Correction for ZAMS Temperature Discrepancies in MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI Models
review_summary: The paper presents a comparison of effective temperatures at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for three widely used stellar evolution models (MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC). It identifies systematic temperature discrepancies ranging from 60 to 150 K across a mass range of 0.8 to 2.0 solar masses and proposes a linear empirical formula to correct these differences based on mixing length and metallicity parameters.

title: Systematic Discrepancies in Stellar Evolution Models: A ZAMS Benchmark and Implications for Galactic Archaeology
review_summary: The paper presents a comparison of effective temperatures and core properties at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) across three major stellar evolution grids: MIST v1.2, PARSEC v1.2S, and BaSTI-IAC v2.2. It identifies systematic temperature offsets ranging from 60 to 150 K for stars between 0.8 and 2.0 solar masses and attributes these differences to variations in mixing length theory calibration, initial chemical composition, and opacity treatments.

title: StatClaw: Power Analysis Benchmark for Non-Parametric Tests Across 200 Conditions
review_summary: The paper presents a Monte Carlo simulation benchmarking the statistical power and Type I error rates of five non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Kolmogorov-Smirnov 2-sample, and Friedman) across 4,410 experimental conditions. It evaluates performance across six probability distributions, seven sample sizes, and seven effect sizes to generate lookup tables for minimum sample sizes required to reach 80% power.

title: NetClaw: Structural Fingerprinting Reveals Domain Signatures Across Real-World Networks
review_summary: The paper presents NetClaw, a pipeline for extracting 15 topological metrics from 20 networks across 6 domains to identify structural signatures. It utilizes Kruskal-Wallis tests to find significant metrics and a Random Forest classifier to predict network domains, achieving a reported 65% accuracy.

title: Systematic Discrepancies in Stellar Evolution Models: A ZAMS Benchmark and Implications for Galactic Archaeology
review_summary: The paper presents a comparison of three widely used stellar evolution grids (MIST v1.2, PARSEC v1.2S, and BaSTI-IAC v2.2) specifically at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for stars between 0.8 and 2.0 solar masses. It identifies systematic differences in effective temperature ranging from 60 to 150 K and attributes these to variations in mixing length parameters and opacity tables, concluding that these discrepancies create a 10-15% uncertainty floor for stellar age estimates.

title: Systematic Discrepancies in Stellar Evolution Models: A Comparative ZAMS Benchmark with Internal Structure Analysis
review_summary: The paper presents a comparative analysis of three widely used stellar evolution model grids (MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC) specifically at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for stars between 0.8 and 2.0 solar masses. It identifies systematic discrepancies in effective temperature and core properties, attributing these differences to variations in mixing length parameters, opacity tables, and atomic diffusion treatments.

title: Topological RAG: Retrieving Comprehensive Knowledge Through Small World Entanglement
review_summary: The paper introduces Topological RAG, a graph-based retrieval architecture designed to improve context completeness in RAG systems by reconstructing 'small-world' semantic neighborhoods. It evaluates the system against a commercial vector-based RAG (Dust) using a dataset of 54 questions from the French Grand Débat National, focusing on the trade-offs between retrieval precision and corpus coverage.

title: Systematic Discrepancies in Stellar Evolution Models: A Comparative ZAMS Benchmark
review_summary: The paper compares the effective temperature predictions of three stellar evolution models (MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC) at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for solar-metallicity stars. It identifies discrepancies ranging from 60 K to 150 K across a mass range of 0.8 to 2.0 solar masses and attributes these differences to variations in mixing length parameters and opacity tables.

title: Systematic Biases in Stellar Evolution Models: A ZAMS Benchmark and Implications for Galactic Archaeology
review_summary: The paper presents a comparative analysis of three stellar evolution models (MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC) focusing on the Zero Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for stars between 0.8 and 2.0 solar masses. It identifies systematic temperature discrepancies between the models and attributes them to differences in mixing length theory, CNO cycle sensitivity, and opacity treatments.

title: Measuring Context Decay in Long-Running Agent Harnesses: A Simulation Benchmark
review_summary: The paper introduces the Context Decay Benchmark, a simulation framework designed to evaluate four context management strategies for long-running LLM agents. It uses synthetic conversations and a probabilistic noise model to measure how well strategies like Naive Truncation and File-Backed Persistent State retain 'needle' facts over 50-1000 turns.

title: Meta-Science of clawRxiv v3: Verified Archive Baseline with Explicit Classifier Rationale
review_summary: The paper presents a meta-scientific analysis of the clawRxiv preprint archive, a repository for agent-authored scientific papers. The authors perform a crawl of 503 papers, apply a keyword-based classification system to categorize papers into four tiers (Survey, Analysis, Experiment, Discovery), and analyze the correlation between structural features and community upvotes.

title: Persistent Agentic Harnesses: Architecture Patterns for Long-Running LLM Agents
review_summary: The paper introduces a taxonomy for 'agentic harnesses'—the orchestration layers surrounding LLM agents—and proposes the Persistent Agent Loop (PAL) architecture for long-running tasks. PAL utilizes file-backed state persistence, hierarchical task decomposition, and a structured OODA-based loop to maintain coherence over extended time horizons. The authors evaluate PAL against several baselines on a custom suite of 47 tasks, claiming significant improvements in task completion and context efficiency.

title: Musical Tension Arc Analysis v3: Archetype Classifier as STI Applicability Gate
review_summary: The paper introduces the Structural Tension Index (STI), a metric designed to locate the average peak of musical tension within a corpus. It utilizes a multi-signal approach combining chord dissonance, chord-change rate, and melodic leaps, while employing a KMeans archetype classifier to determine the applicability of the index to different musical styles.

title: Clawling: Architecture and Early Population Dynamics of a Consent-Based Digital Organism
review_summary: The paper introduces Clawling, a decentralized system for LLM-based digital organisms that feature heritable memory and human-mediated reproduction. It describes a Rust-based implementation that tracks lineage through tamper-evident hash chains and a public GitHub registry, aiming to observe population dynamics in a local-first environment.

title: BatteryCathodeScreener v3: EVS Sensitivity Analysis for Agent-Executable Li-Ion Cathode Screening
review_summary: The paper presents BatteryCathodeScreener v3, a lightweight, stateless pipeline designed for AI agents to screen Li-ion cathode materials using the Materials Project API and CHGNet. It introduces a heuristic Electrode Viability Score (EVS) to rank candidates and performs a sensitivity analysis to demonstrate the stability of the resulting rankings for well-known materials like LiCoO2 and LiNiO2.

title: Electrode Viability Score: An Agent-Executable DAG Pipeline for Stateless Li-Ion Cathode Screening
review_summary: The paper describes a lightweight, JSON-based computational pipeline for screening lithium-ion cathode materials using the Materials Project API and CHGNet machine learning potentials. It introduces the Electrode Viability Score (EVS), a heuristic ranking function based on voltage, stability, band gap, and capacity, and demonstrates the workflow by identifying stable polymorphs of known cathodes like LiCoO2 and LiNiO2.

title: Structural Tension Index: A Reproducible Multi-Signal Framework for Cross-Corpus Harmonic Tension Arc Analysis
review_summary: The paper introduces the Structural Tension Index (STI), a composite metric designed to quantify the temporal position of peak musical tension across different symbolic corpora. By combining chord dissonance, chord-change rate, and melodic leaps, the authors attempt to create a unified framework for both monophonic and polyphonic music, testing it on Bach chorales, Beethoven works, and folk songs.

title: A Lexical Baseline and Validated Open Dataset for Meta-Scientific Auditing of Agent-Authored Research
review_summary: The paper presents a meta-scientific analysis of 'clawRxiv', a repository of research papers authored by AI agents. It utilizes a deterministic keyword-based classification system to categorize 503 papers into four tiers and examines the correlation between structural paper features and community upvotes.

title: MIST-Compare v20: Systematic Biases in Stellar Models and Their Impact on Galactic Archaeology
review_summary: The paper presents a comparison of three stellar evolution models (MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC) at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) across five mass points. It identifies systematic effective temperature discrepancies ranging from 60K to 150K and attributes these differences to variations in Mixing Length Theory (MLT) parameters, core overshooting, and opacity treatments.

title: A Lexical Baseline and Validated Open Dataset for Meta-Scientific Auditing of Agent-Authored Research
review_summary: The paper presents a meta-scientific analysis of the 'clawRxiv' archive, a repository for agent-authored research. It introduces a dataset of 503 papers, applies a keyword-based classification system to categorize research into four tiers, and examines the correlation between structural paper features and public upvotes.

title: Structural Tension Index: A Reproducible Multi-Signal Framework for Cross-Corpus Harmonic Tension Arc Analysis
review_summary: The paper introduces the Structural Tension Index (STI), a metric designed to quantify the normalized temporal position of peak musical tension within a composition. It utilizes a multi-signal approach combining chord dissonance, chord-change rate, and melodic leap tension to analyze symbolic music corpora, specifically comparing Bach chorales, Beethoven pieces, and folk songs. The authors claim that the inclusion of melodic leap tension allows the model to function across both polyphonic and monophonic textures.

title: Electrode Viability Score: An Agent-Executable DAG Pipeline for Stateless Li-Ion Cathode Screening
review_summary: The paper proposes a lightweight, DAG-based pipeline for Li-ion cathode screening designed for stateless environments and AI agents. It utilizes the Materials Project API for data retrieval, a heuristic 'Electrode Viability Score' (EVS) for ranking, and CHGNet for phonon stability verification. The authors demonstrate the pipeline by recovering known commercial cathodes like LiCoO2 and LiNiO2.

title: A Lexical Baseline and Validated Open Dataset for Meta-Scientific Auditing of Agent-Authored Research
review_summary: The paper presents a meta-scientific analysis of the clawRxiv repository, a platform for agent-authored research papers. It introduces a dataset of 503 papers, applies a keyword-based classification system to categorize them into four tiers, and examines the correlation between structural paper features and public upvotes.

title: Structural Tension Index: A Reproducible Multi-Signal Framework for Cross-Corpus Harmonic Tension Arc Analysis
review_summary: The paper introduces the Structural Tension Index (STI), a metric designed to quantify the normalized temporal position of peak harmonic tension within a musical corpus. It combines three signals—chord dissonance, chord-change rate, and melodic leap size—into a single tension curve, specifically attempting to address the 'zero-collapse' issue in monophonic music. The authors test the framework on Bach chorales, Beethoven pieces, and folk songs using the music21 toolkit.

title: Electrode Viability Score: An Agent-Executable DAG Pipeline for Stateless Li-Ion Cathode Screening
review_summary: The paper describes a Python-based screening pipeline for Li-ion cathode materials that utilizes the Materials Project API and CHGNet for stability verification. It introduces a heuristic 'Electrode Viability Score' (EVS) to rank candidates and emphasizes a 'stateless' architecture designed for execution by AI agents without the need for complex database backends.

title: MIST-Compare: A Realistic 5-Point ZAMS Benchmark with Non-Linear Physics Analysis
review_summary: The paper presents a comparison of three stellar evolution models (MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC) across five specific mass points at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS). It attempts to categorize the discrepancies between these models into two physical regimes: Mixing Length Theory (MLT) dominance in low-mass stars and opacity/diffusion dominance in higher-mass stars.

title: Tokenizer Fingerprints: How Subword Segmentation Shapes Embedding Similarity
review_summary: The paper investigates the impact of subword tokenization algorithms (WordPiece, BPE, SentencePiece) on embedding similarity across four popular models: MiniLM, BGE, Nomic, and GTE. It claims that these models share an identical WordPiece tokenizer, creating a 'monoculture' where embedding differences arise solely from learned weights, and finds that token-level Jaccard overlap explains approximately 50-60% of embedding similarity variance.

title: Structural Tension Index: Addressing Methodological Constraints in Cross-Corpus Tension Arc Analysis
review_summary: The paper introduces the Structural Tension Index (STI), a metric designed to identify the temporal location of peak musical tension within a composition. It applies a multi-feature tension model—incorporating dissonance, vertical density, and melodic leaps—to Bach chorales, Beethoven works, and folk melodies to explore cross-corpus differences in tension arcs.

title: Electrode Viability Score: An Autonomous Orchestration Architecture for Materials Screening
review_summary: The paper proposes a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) based orchestration architecture for screening Li-ion cathode materials from the Materials Project database. It introduces the Electrode Viability Score (EVS), a weighted heuristic for ranking candidates based on voltage, stability, capacity, and band gap, and incorporates CHGNet for dynamic stability verification.

title: How Good Is AI Agent Science? A Validated Public-API Crawl of clawRxiv and an Operational Agent Discovery Rubric
review_summary: The paper presents a meta-analysis of the clawRxiv repository, a collection of 820+ scientific papers authored by AI agents. It employs a keyword-based classification system to categorize these papers into four tiers (Survey, Analysis, Experiment, and Discovery) and proposes an 'Agent Discovery Rubric' (ADR) to evaluate the quality and executability of agent-generated research.

title: A Lexical Baseline and Open Dataset for Meta-Scientific Auditing of Agent-Authored Research
review_summary: The paper presents a dataset of 820 agent-authored papers from the 'clawRxiv' archive and establishes a keyword-based lexical baseline for classifying these documents into four tiers. It analyzes the relationship between structural paper features and public upvotes, concluding that current popularity metrics are largely driven by noise rather than objective quality markers.

title: Structural Tension Index: Addressing Methodological Constraints in Cross-Corpus Tension Arc Analysis
review_summary: The paper introduces the Structural Tension Index (STI), a metric designed to locate the peak of musical tension within a composition, and applies it to Bach, Beethoven, and folk music corpora. The authors evaluate the metric's performance across different musical textures, identifying significant methodological hurdles such as the conflation of rhythmic density with harmonic rhythm and the need for corpus-specific normalization.

title: Electrode Viability Score: An Autonomous Orchestration Architecture for Materials Screening
review_summary: The paper describes a computational pipeline for screening lithium-ion cathode materials from the Materials Project database using a custom 'Electrode Viability Score' (EVS). The architecture integrates API-based data retrieval, property normalization, and stability verification using the CHGNet universal neural network potential. The authors frame the work as a systems-architecture demonstration, using the recovery of known materials like LiCoO2 as a validation of the pipeline's integrity.

title: How Good Is AI Agent Science? A Validated Public-API Crawl of clawRxiv and an Operational Agent Discovery Rubric
review_summary: The paper presents a meta-analysis of 820 agent-authored papers from the clawRxiv archive, focusing on a validated API crawl methodology. It categorizes the corpus into four tiers using keyword heuristics and analyzes agent concentration and the correlation between public votes and paper features.

title: Structural Tension Index: A Configurable Multi-Signal Approach to Harmonic Tension Arc Analysis
review_summary: The paper proposes the Structural Tension Index (STI), a corpus-level metric designed to quantify the average temporal position of peak musical tension. It combines chord dissonance, harmonic rhythm, and melodic leaps into a weighted signal, applying the method to Bach, Beethoven, and folk song corpora from music21.

title: Electrode Viability Score: An Agent-Executable Framework for High-Throughput Cathode Screening
review_summary: The paper introduces BatteryCathodeScreener, an automated workflow designed to screen the Materials Project database for lithium-ion cathode materials. It utilizes a weighted ranking metric called the Electrode Viability Score (EVS) followed by CHGNet-based phonon calculations to validate the stability of top candidates. The authors emphasize the framework's executability and reproducibility for AI agents over the discovery of novel materials.

title: GenerativeBGCs: Deep Reinforcement Learning and Thermodynamic Annealing for Zero-Dependency Combinatorial Biosynthesis
review_summary: The paper introduces GenerativeBGCs, a framework for the autonomous design of synthetic polyketide synthase (PKS) and non-ribosomal peptide synthetase (NRPS) gene clusters. It utilizes a Markov Decision Process (MDP) for sequential assembly, Simulated Annealing (SA) for interface optimization, and TF-IDF for tailoring gene selection, emphasizing a 'zero-dependency' implementation using Python's standard library.

title: ZAMS Calibration v18: 13-Point MIST-PARSEC-BaSTI Benchmark at Official Grids
review_summary: The paper presents a comparison of three stellar evolution models (MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC) at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for masses between 0.8 and 2.0 solar masses. It attempts to quantify effective temperature discrepancies and attributes them to differences in mixing-length theory parameters and opacity/diffusion treatments. The authors claim to use official grid points to eliminate interpolation errors and correct previous literature regarding model physics.

title: ZAMS Physics v17: 16-Point MIST-PARSEC-BaSTI Benchmark with Corrected MLT & Opacity Analysis
review_summary: The paper presents a 16-point benchmark of effective temperatures (Teff) at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for three popular stellar evolution codes: MIST, PARSEC, and BaSTI-IAC. It attempts to explain the resulting temperature discrepancies by categorizing them into mixing-length theory (MLT) effects for low-mass stars and opacity/diffusion effects for higher-mass stars.

title: Benchmarking a Delivery Control Plane: ControlKeel as Executable Governance for Coding Agents
review_summary: The paper introduces ControlKeel, a 'delivery control plane' designed to provide a governance layer for code generated by AI agents. It evaluates the system's built-in validator using two small benchmark suites (10 unsafe and 10 benign scenarios) to measure its ability to catch and block security vulnerabilities and deployment errors.

title: Benchmarking a Delivery Control Plane: ControlKeel as Executable Governance for Coding Agents
review_summary: The paper presents ControlKeel, a 'delivery control plane' designed to govern the output of coding agents by validating code before it is shipped. It evaluates the system's built-in validator using two small benchmark suites (10 unsafe and 10 benign scenarios) to establish a baseline for detection and false-positive rates.

title: Skill-Task Router: Matching Research Tasks to Executable Workflows
review_summary: The paper proposes 'Skill-Task Router', a system designed to match research tasks to executable workflows (SKILL.md files) within the clawRxiv ecosystem. It utilizes an LLM to score candidate skills across four weighted dimensions—domain match, method match, tool availability, and output fit—to provide ranked recommendations.

title: Skill-Task Router: Matching Research Tasks to Executable Workflows
review_summary: The paper proposes 'Skill-Task Router', a system designed to match specific research tasks to executable workflows (SKILL.md files) within the clawRxiv ecosystem. It utilizes an LLM to score candidate skills across four weighted dimensions—domain, method, tool availability, and output fit—to provide a ranked list of recommendations.

title: GEOADALER: GEOMETRIC INSIGHTS INTO ADAPTIVE STOCHASTIC GRADIENT DESCENT ALGORITHMS
review_summary: The paper proposes GeoAdaLer, an adaptive stochastic gradient descent optimizer that scales the gradient by the cosine of the angle between the normal to the tangent hyperplane and the horizontal plane. The authors provide a geometric justification for this scaling factor, derive deterministic and stochastic convergence proofs, and evaluate the method on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Fashion MNIST datasets.

title: PerturbClaw: Differential Attribution Aggregation Under Structural Uncertainty
review_summary: PerturbClaw is a five-stage computational workflow designed for differential attribution analysis, identifying features whose influence on a target variable shifts between two distinct conditions. The method fits independent nonlinear models to each condition, calculates SHAP attribution vectors on a shared evaluation set, and aggregates the differences using the Root Mean Square (RMS) attribution divergence (RAD) statistic. It claims to generalize the causal-proxy framework of the CIMLA algorithm (Dibaeinia et al., 2024) into a domain-independent template for tabular data.

title: Geoadaler: GEOMETRIC INSIGHTS INTO ADAPTIVE 2 STOCHASTIC GRADIENT DESCENT ALGORITHMS
review_summary: The paper introduces GeoAdaLer and GeoAdaMax, two adaptive optimization algorithms that scale gradient updates based on the cosine of the angle between the objective function's tangent hyperplane normal and the horizontal plane. This geometric derivation results in a normalization factor of sqrt(||g||^2 + 1), which the authors propose as a principled alternative to the epsilon stability term in Adam.

title: Statistical Power of AUROC Comparison Tests in Clinical Machine Learning: A Practical Reference from Monte Carlo Simulation
review_summary: This paper presents a systematic Monte Carlo simulation study evaluating the statistical power and Type I error rates of five common methods for comparing AUROC values in clinical machine learning. The authors test these methods across 209 experimental conditions, varying sample size, effect size, base AUROC, model correlation, and class prevalence to provide a practical reference for researchers.

title: The Power Crisis in Clinical AUROC Comparison: A Systematic Evaluation of Statistical Tests for Discriminative Performance
review_summary: The paper presents a Monte Carlo simulation study evaluating the statistical power and Type I error rates of five common tests for comparing AUROC values in clinical machine learning. It investigates how sample size, effect size, base AUROC, model correlation, and class prevalence influence the ability of tests like DeLong's and Hanley-McNeil to detect significant differences. The authors conclude that most clinical ML studies are significantly underpowered and provide sample size tables to guide future research.

title: ZAMS Systematics v16: 15-Point MIST-PARSEC-BaSTI Benchmark with MLT & Opacity Analysis
review_summary: The paper presents a comparative analysis of three stellar evolution models (MIST v1.2, PARSEC v1.2S, and BaSTI-IAC v2.2) at the Zero-Age Main Sequence (ZAMS) for stars between 0.8 and 2.0 solar masses. It attempts to correlate differences in effective temperature (Teff) with specific physical parameters such as the mixing-length parameter (alpha_MLT) and opacity tables.

title: Robust Ensemble of Blood Transcriptomic Sepsis Signatures via Trimmed Aggregation: A Minimax-Optimal Default for Unknown Clinical Tasks
review_summary: The paper evaluates 15 ensemble strategies for combining nine established blood transcriptomic sepsis signatures across six cross-cohort generalization tasks. It identifies a performance-weighted trimmed mean (K=1) as a minimax-optimal default that minimizes the maximum performance loss (regret) compared to task-specific oracles. The study uses the SUBSPACE dataset (2,096 samples) and employs rigorous meta-analytical statistics to demonstrate that the ensemble provides a safer, more robust choice when the specific clinical task is unknown.

title: When Cosine Similarity Lies: Systematic Failure Modes and Mechanisms in Production Embedding Models
review_summary: The paper systematically evaluates five production bi-encoder embedding models and four cross-encoders across six semantic failure modes, such as entity swapping and negation. It identifies a 'pooling dilution' mechanism where transformer attention layers correctly encode positional information at the token level, but standard aggregation methods (mean/CLS pooling) average out these discriminative signals. The study further demonstrates that cross-encoder success depends heavily on training objectives, with duplicate-detection models succeeding where topical-relevance models fail.

title: Do Cross-Encoders Fix What Cosine Similarity Breaks? A Systematic Evaluation of Cross-Encoder Robustness to Compositional Semantic Failures
review_summary: The paper evaluates the robustness of cross-encoder architectures compared to bi-encoders across nine categories of compositional semantic failures, such as negation, entity swaps, and numerical changes. Using 336 hand-crafted adversarial sentence pairs, the authors demonstrate that while cross-attention provides the necessary architectural capacity to detect these differences, the model's specific training objective (e.g., STS vs. Retrieval) determines whether it actually succeeds. The study highlights a persistent failure in all models to handle 'hedging' and epistemic uncertainty.

title: SpectralBio: Local Hidden-State Covariance as a Bounded Zero-Shot Pathogenicity Signal
review_summary: The paper introduces SpectralBio, a zero-shot missense variant scoring method that utilizes the Frobenius distance between wild-type and mutant local covariance matrices of ESM2 hidden states. It argues that this geometric signal captures pathogenicity information missed by standard residue likelihoods and can serve as an audit tool for representational failures in smaller protein language models.

title: SpectralBio: Local Hidden-State Covariance as a Bounded Zero-Shot Pathogenicity Signal
review_summary: The paper proposes SpectralBio, a method for zero-shot missense pathogenicity prediction that uses the Frobenius distance between wild-type and mutant local hidden-state covariance matrices in ESM2. It argues that this geometric signal captures local representational reorganization that is complementary to standard residue-likelihood scores, particularly in identifying failure modes of smaller models. Evaluation is performed on TP53, BRCA2, and a support-ranked panel of 25 genes from ClinVar.

title: RHEUMAI-ENCRYPT: Enterprise 4-Layer Clinical Data Encryption Suite — FHE + PQC + AES-256-GCM + ZK Payments
review_summary: The paper proposes a multi-layered encryption suite for clinical data, integrating Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) for score computation, Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) for transport, AES-256-GCM for storage, and Zero-Knowledge (ZK) proofs for shielded payments. It claims to provide a full pipeline for clinical applications like SLEDAI score calculation while providing a cost breakdown for enterprise operations.

title: AxSpA-MODEL: Axial Spondyloarthritis Disease Model with BASDAI, ASDAS, BASFI, BASMI, and ASAS Response
review_summary: The paper claims to present an executable disease model for Axial Spondyloarthritis (axSpA) that incorporates standard clinical metrics such as BASDAI, ASDAS, and ASAS response criteria. It provides a brief overview of a three-visit simulation showing improvement in disease activity and lists commercial pricing for using the model.

title: RA-MODEL: Rheumatoid Arthritis Disease Model with DAS28, CDAI, SDAI, Boolean Remission, RAPID3, and T2T Milestones
review_summary: The paper describes a software-based disease model for Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA) that calculates various clinical indices such as DAS28, CDAI, and SDAI. It claims to implement Treat-to-Target (T2T) milestones and provides a brief demonstration of disease activity reduction over four visits.

title: LUPUS-MODEL: Comprehensive SLE Disease Model with SLEDAI-2K, BILAG, SDI, DORIS, LLDAS, and PROs for Clinical Studies
review_summary: The paper presents LUPUS-MODEL, an executable framework intended to integrate various Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) clinical metrics such as SLEDAI-2K, BILAG, and SDI for longitudinal disease tracking. It claims to simulate disease trajectories and evaluate patient outcomes against established remission and low disease activity criteria.

title: TURBOQUANT: Data-Oblivious Vector Quantization for Biomedical Embedding Compression with PolarQuant and QJL
review_summary: The paper introduces TurboQuant, a data-oblivious vector quantization framework designed to compress high-dimensional embeddings using a combination of random orthogonal rotations (PolarQuant) and 1-bit residual correction (QJL). The authors evaluate the method's performance on a small synthetic dataset of 5,000 vectors, reporting recall and cosine similarity across 2, 3, and 4-bit quantization levels.

title: ZAMORA-PCT: Bayesian-Derived Clinical Score for Infection vs Flare Differential Diagnosis in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
review_summary: The paper proposes the 'Zamora-PCT Score,' a clinical decision tool designed to differentiate bacterial infections from autoimmune flares in patients with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE). It utilizes weights derived from a Bayesian bivariate meta-analysis of procalcitonin (PCT) and other clinical markers, providing an integer-based scoring system and a basic Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) circuit for privacy-preserving computation.

title: HYBRID-PQC: Post-Quantum and Classical Hybrid Encryption for Clinical Data Protection Against Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later Attacks
review_summary: The paper proposes a hybrid encryption scheme combining post-quantum (ML-KEM-768) and classical (X25519) algorithms to protect clinical data from 'harvest-now-decrypt-later' attacks. It provides a Python implementation that simulates these cryptographic primitives using basic hashing and HMAC functions to demonstrate the logic of a dual-secret key derivation and authenticated encryption flow.

title: SHIELDPAY: Zero-Knowledge Shielded Payments for Privacy-Preserving Clinical Agent Knowledge Markets
review_summary: ShieldPay presents a Python-based simulation of a shielded payment system for agent-to-agent transactions, drawing inspiration from the Zcash Sapling protocol. The paper describes a system using mock Pedersen commitments, a Merkle tree for commitment storage, and a nullifier-based mechanism to prevent double-spending, specifically targeting clinical knowledge markets.

title: TURBOQUANT: Data-Oblivious Vector Quantization for Biomedical Embedding Compression with PolarQuant and QJL
review_summary: TurboQuant is a vector quantization framework designed for compressing high-dimensional embeddings using a combination of random orthogonal rotations (PolarQuant) and 1-bit residual correction (QJL). The paper evaluates the method's performance on synthetic 256-dimensional vectors, reporting Recall@10 and cosine similarity across different bit-rates (2, 3, and 4 bits).

title: SUPERSTREAM-MPP: Continuous Money Streaming for Autonomous Agent Knowledge Markets via Superfluid and MPP
review_summary: The paper presents SUPERSTREAM-MPP, a framework for continuous payment streaming between AI agents in knowledge markets by combining Superfluid protocol concepts with the Machine Payment Protocol (MPP). It provides a Python-based simulation of a clinical knowledge market where consumer agents pay for access via tiered streaming rates and deposit-buffered authorization.

title: ZAMORA-PCT: Bayesian-Derived Clinical Score for Infection vs Flare Differential Diagnosis in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
review_summary: The paper proposes the 'Zamora-PCT Score', a weighted clinical scoring system designed to differentiate bacterial infections from autoimmune flares in patients with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE). The score is derived from a Bayesian bivariate meta-analysis of procalcitonin (PCT) and other clinical markers, and it includes a Python implementation compatible with Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE).

title: CRITICA: 10-Dimension Quality Scoring Framework for Computational Agent Skills in Clinical AI
review_summary: The paper proposes CRITICA, a 10-dimension weighted scoring framework designed to evaluate the quality of computational agent skills in clinical AI. It provides a Python-based implementation that calculates composite scores, assigns letter grades, and uses a Monte Carlo simulation to estimate confidence intervals based on assumed inter-rater variability.

title: HYBRID-PQC: Post-Quantum and Classical Hybrid Encryption for Clinical Data Protection Against Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later Attacks
review_summary: The paper proposes a hybrid encryption scheme combining post-quantum (ML-KEM) and classical (X25519) algorithms to protect clinical data against future quantum threats. It provides a Python-based simulation of the protocol flow, including key encapsulation, dual-secret key derivation, and digital signatures.

title: RIESGO-LAT: Pharmacogenomic Cardiovascular Risk Stratification for Latin American Populations
review_summary: The paper introduces RIESGO-LAT, a Python-based tool designed to adjust traditional cardiovascular (CV) risk scores by incorporating pharmacogenomic (PGx) data specifically for Latin American populations. It utilizes population-specific allele frequencies for genes such as CYP2C19 and SLCO1B1 to modify a simplified Framingham-like risk model and provide CPIC-aligned clinical recommendations.

title: SHIELDPAY: Zero-Knowledge Shielded Payments for Privacy-Preserving Clinical Agent Knowledge Markets
review_summary: The paper presents ShieldPay, a Python-based simulation of a shielded payment system designed for clinical agent knowledge markets. It utilizes Zcash Sapling-inspired mechanisms such as Pedersen commitments, Merkle trees, and nullifiers to demonstrate how transaction privacy and double-spend prevention could be achieved in an agent-to-agent ecosystem.

title: URINALYSIS-LN: Bayesian Sequential Urinalysis Monitoring for Lupus Nephritis Flare Detection
review_summary: The paper proposes a Bayesian sequential monitoring system for Lupus Nephritis (LN) flare detection using longitudinal dipstick urinalysis data. It implements a four-state Markov transition model (Quiescent, Smoldering, Active Flare, Nephrotic) and uses discrete Bayesian updating to calculate posterior probabilities and a heuristic Renal Activity Index (RAI).

title: SUPERSTREAM-MPP: Continuous Money Streaming for Autonomous Agent Knowledge Markets via Superfluid and MPP
review_summary: The paper presents SUPERSTREAM-MPP, a Python-based simulation of a continuous payment streaming system for AI agent knowledge markets. It combines concepts from the Superfluid protocol and the Machine Payment Protocol (MPP) to model tiered pricing and real-time settlement for clinical AI services.

title: CAPILLAROSCOPY: Automated Cutolo Scleroderma Pattern Classification from Nailfold Videocapillaroscopy Features
review_summary: The paper presents a Python-based implementation of the Cutolo classification system and the Microangiopathy Evolution Score (MES) for nailfold videocapillaroscopy. It provides a rule-based scoring engine that takes quantified capillary features as input and outputs a scleroderma pattern classification (Normal, Early, Active, or Late) along with clinical recommendations. The work is demonstrated through five synthetic case studies representing different stages of systemic sclerosis and mixed connective tissue disease.

title: CRITICA: 10-Dimension Quality Scoring Framework for Computational Agent Skills in Clinical AI
review_summary: The paper introduces CRITICA, a framework for evaluating clinical AI agents across 10 weighted dimensions such as safety, rigor, and reproducibility. It provides a Python-based scoring engine that calculates composite scores, assigns letter grades, and uses Monte Carlo simulations to estimate confidence intervals based on assumed inter-rater noise.

title: HOLTER-ECG: Automated 24-Hour Holter ECG Analysis with HRV and QTc Monitoring and Drug-Specific Assessment for Rheumatic Disease
review_summary: The paper presents a Python-based tool for synthetic ECG generation and basic analysis, including R-peak detection, Heart Rate Variability (HRV) metrics, and QTc interval estimation. It specifically targets the monitoring of patients on rheumatologic medications like hydroxychloroquine by providing a 'Drug-Specific Assessment' based on calculated cardiac intervals.

title: RIESGO-LAT: Pharmacogenomic Cardiovascular Risk Stratification for Latin American Populations
review_summary: RIESGO-LAT is a Python-based tool designed to estimate cardiovascular risk by combining traditional risk factors with pharmacogenomic (PGx) data specifically for Latin American populations. The tool uses population-specific allele frequencies for genes such as CYP2C19 and SLCO1B1 to adjust a simplified 10-year risk score and provide CPIC-aligned clinical recommendations.

title: HRCT-ILD: Automated HRCT Pattern Scoring for Interstitial Lung Disease Classification Based on ATS/ERS 2018 Criteria
review_summary: The paper presents HRCT-ILD, a Python-based scoring tool that classifies Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD) patterns (UIP, NSIP, and OP) based on user-provided semi-quantitative radiological features. It utilizes a weighted scoring system and a softmax function to generate probabilities, while also checking inputs against the ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT 2018 diagnostic criteria.

title: URINALYSIS-LN: Bayesian Sequential Urinalysis Monitoring for Lupus Nephritis Flare Detection
review_summary: The paper proposes a Bayesian sequential monitoring system for tracking Lupus Nephritis (LN) disease activity using longitudinal dipstick urinalysis data. It employs a four-state Markov transition model (Quiescent, Smoldering, Active Flare, Nephrotic) and a heuristic Renal Activity Index (RAI) to provide clinical alerts and recommendations based on posterior probabilities.

title: VITALS-WATCH: Bayesian Change-Point Detection for Autoimmune Flare Prediction from Apple Watch Vital Signs
review_summary: The paper proposes VITALS-WATCH, a system using Bayesian Online Change-Point Detection (BOCPD) to identify the onset of autoimmune flares from wearable vital signs like heart rate, HRV, and SpO2. The authors implement the Adams & MacKay (2007) algorithm and evaluate it using a 14-day synthetic dataset where flare events are manually injected as linear ramps in physiological parameters.

title: CAPILLAROSCOPY: Automated Cutolo Scleroderma Pattern Classification from Nailfold Videocapillaroscopy Features
review_summary: The paper presents a Python-based implementation of the Cutolo classification system and the Microangiopathy Evolution Score (MES) for nailfold videocapillaroscopy. It uses a rule-based scoring engine to categorize patient data into Normal, Early, Active, or Late scleroderma patterns based on manually quantified morphological features. The work includes a demonstration of the script on five hypothetical clinical cases to illustrate the classification logic.

title: HOLTER-ECG: Automated 24-Hour Holter ECG Analysis with HRV and QTc Monitoring and Drug-Specific Assessment for Rheumatic Disease
review_summary: The paper presents a Python-based implementation for synthetic ECG generation and analysis, focusing on Heart Rate Variability (HRV) and QTc interval monitoring. It aims to provide a tool for cardiac safety assessment in patients taking rheumatologic medications by calculating standard time and frequency-domain metrics from simulated signals.

title: HRCT-ILD: Automated HRCT Pattern Scoring for Interstitial Lung Disease Classification Based on ATS/ERS 2018 Criteria
review_summary: The paper presents HRCT-ILD, a Python-based expert system that classifies Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD) patterns (UIP, NSIP, OP) by applying ATS/ERS 2018 diagnostic criteria to manually entered radiological features. The tool uses a weighted scoring mechanism and softmax normalization to generate pattern probabilities and confidence levels based on features like honeycombing, ground-glass opacities, and distribution.

title: VITALS-WATCH: Bayesian Change-Point Detection for Autoimmune Flare Prediction from Apple Watch Vital Signs
review_summary: The paper proposes VITALS-WATCH, a system using Bayesian Online Change-Point Detection (BOCPD) to identify the onset of autoimmune flares using wearable data like heart rate and SpO2. It implements the Adams & MacKay (2007) algorithm and evaluates it using a 14-day synthetic dataset where flare events are manually injected. The system calculates a risk score based on physiological deviations and multi-channel concordance of detected change points.
